name: System Design — Distributed Systems
description: The bedrock primitives: time, ordering, failure detection, leader election, quorums, consensus, gossip, idempotency, fencing, sagas.

cards.definition: 1) Networks fail asymmetrically — packets dropped, delayed, duplicated, reordered. Can't tell 'slow' from 'dead'. 2) Clocks disagree even with NTP. 3) Partial failure is the default. Every distributed pattern is a workaround for one of these.
Never rely on wall-clock comparisons across machines for correctness. NTP keeps clocks within ~10-100 ms; clocks can also jump backward. The Cloudflare 2017 leap-second bug is the canonical incident.

A counter per process. Local event: counter++. On send: attach counter. On receive(c): counter = max(counter, c) + 1. Guarantees: if a → b, then LC(a) < LC(b). Converse not true — smaller counter doesn't prove causal order.

Each process maintains V[i] for every process i. Local event: V[me]++. On receive: for all i, V[i] = max(V[i], received V[i]); then V[me]++. Correctly distinguishes 'a happened before b', 'b before a', and 'concurrent'. Used in Dynamo to detect concurrent writes.

Wall-clock time + a logical counter. Sortable by physical time but tolerant of clock skew within a bounded window. Used by CockroachDB and YugabyteDB. Practical compromise between Lamport and Spanner's TrueTime.

cards.term: Failure detection is suspicion, not fact
cards.definition: You can never be sure a remote node is dead — only that you haven't heard from it. Every protocol must be safe even if a 'dead' node is actually slow and comes back. Detection time vs false positives is the core tradeoff.
Builds a continuous suspicion score from heartbeat history (instead of binary alive/dead). Adapts to network jitter — a slow link doesn't trip false alarms. Used in Cassandra.

Each node periodically picks a few random peers and exchanges state. Information spreads exponentially: O(log N) rounds reach all nodes. Scales to thousands; eventually consistent. Used for membership in Cassandra, Consul, Serf.
Highest-ID live node wins the election. Classic textbook protocol; rare in production because it's chatty and assumes synchronous communication.

A node holds a time-limited lease (e.g. via etcd or ZooKeeper); it renews before expiry. If it can't renew, it stops acting as leader. Whoever grabs the lock next takes over. Used by Kubernetes leader-election and ZooKeeper.

A network partition lets two nodes both believe they're the leader. Both accept writes; data diverges. Defenses: require quorum to elect (so both partitions can't both have a quorum) and use fencing tokens to reject stale-leader writes.

A monotonically increasing number issued with each lock grant. Downstream resources only accept writes with the highest token they've seen — rejecting writes from a process whose lease silently expired (GC pause, partition).
A subset of nodes large enough that any two quorums must overlap. Overlap guarantees that a value seen by one quorum is also seen by any subsequent quorum.
For N nodes, quorum = ⌊N/2⌋ + 1 (majority). Tolerates ⌊(N−1)/2⌋ failures. N=3 → quorum 2, tolerates 1. N=5 → quorum 3, tolerates 2. N=7 → quorum 4, tolerates 3.

Tolerates nodes that lie or send corrupt data. Quorum = ⌊2N/3⌋ + 1; needs at least 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine nodes. Most backend systems assume crash-stop; Byzantine-tolerant protocols (PBFT, HotStuff) appear in blockchains.
W=N, R=1: strong reads, slow writes. W=1, R=N: fast writes, slow reads, still strong. W=⌈N/2⌉+1, R=⌈N/2⌉+1: balanced; survives ⌊(N−1)/2⌋ failures. Cassandra/DynamoDB expose W and R per query.

Get N nodes to agree on a single value (or sequence) despite failures. Foundation of replicated state machines, distributed locks, leader election. Solved by Paxos (1989, hard) and Raft (2014, designed to be understandable).

Splits consensus into leader election, log replication, and safety. Leader takes all writes, replicates to followers; commit when a majority acks. On leader death, a new term begins and a new leader is elected. Used by etcd, Consul, CockroachDB.

In an asynchronous system with even one faulty process, no deterministic protocol can guarantee consensus. Real systems work around this with timeouts (giving up on async perfection in exchange for liveness).

Every write costs at least one quorum-RTT. Use it for the small set of decisions that must be globally agreed (cluster membership, config, lock grants). Don't run every business write through Raft — replicate state separately.

Some operations are idempotent by nature: SET x=5 (vs INCREMENT x). HTTP PUT and DELETE are; POST is not. For non-natural ops (charge a payment), the client passes a unique idempotency key the server uses to dedupe retries.

Store (idempotency_key, result) in the DB. On request, check existence — if present, return cached result; else execute, then store. Use a unique index on the key column to prevent races on concurrent retries.
Read with a version number; on write, check the version hasn't changed (UPDATE ... WHERE id=? AND version=?). If it has, retry. No locks held; high throughput. Best when conflicts are rare.
Acquire a lock before reading; hold until commit. Other writers block. Prevents conflicts; reduces throughput; risks deadlocks. Best when conflicts are frequent or the cost of conflict is high.

Closed: normal — calls flow through, failure rate tracked. Open: failure rate exceeded threshold — calls fail immediately for a cooldown. Half-open: timeout elapsed — let a test request through; success → close, failure → reopen.
When a downstream is failing, continuing to call it 1) wastes threads waiting on timeouts, 2) prolongs the downstream's outage, 3) cascades failures upstream. Failing fast contains the blast radius.
Double the wait on each retry: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s. Reduces load during an outage. Without jitter, all clients still synchronize and create waves on recovery.
Add randomness to backoff intervals so retries spread across time, preventing synchronized waves on a recovering service. Standard recommendation for distributed clients (e.g. AWS SDK).
Network timeouts, 503s, 429s — yes. 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 401 Unauthorized — no. Non-idempotent ops should only be retried with an idempotency key. Always set max retries + a deadline.

Each service publishes events; the next service subscribes and reacts. No central coordinator. Pros: low coupling, simple flows. Cons: hard to see overall state; complex flows fragment across services.

A central orchestrator drives the sequence — calls each service, waits for reply, calls compensations on failure. Single source of truth; better for complex/evolving flows. Tradeoff: orchestrator is a coupling hot spot.

Coordinator is a SPOF. Participants hold locks during 'prepare' (blocking other writers). If the coordinator crashes after prepare but before commit, participants block indefinitely waiting for resolution. Why most modern systems prefer sagas.

Nodes don't just stop — they lie, send corrupt data, or behave maliciously. Most backend systems assume crash-stop (nodes fail by halting). Byzantine-tolerance is needed for blockchains and high-assurance systems.
